questions.stem: A tall patient can protrude the thumb beyond the ulnar border of a clenched fist (left) and can overlap the thumb and fifth finger around the opposite wrist (right). These maneuvers are classic bedside signs of which disorder?

explanation.summary: The thumb (Steinberg) sign and wrist (Walker-Murdoch) sign reflect the arachnodactyly and joint laxity of Marfan syndrome, caused by mutations in the FBN1 gene encoding fibrillin-1.
explanation.whyCorrect: The thumb (Steinberg) sign and wrist (Walker-Murdoch) sign reflect the arachnodactyly and joint laxity of Marfan syndrome, caused by mutations in the FBN1 gene encoding fibrillin-1.

questions.stem: These scanning electron micrographs contrast a normal biconcave red cell (a) with the deformed crescent-shaped cell (b). The cell in (b) is characteristic of which condition?

explanation.summary: A point mutation (Glu->Val) in beta-globin produces HbS, which polymerizes when deoxygenated and distorts the erythrocyte into the rigid sickle shape shown in panel b, causing hemolysis and vaso-occlusion.
explanation.whyCorrect: A point mutation (Glu->Val) in beta-globin produces HbS, which polymerizes when deoxygenated and distorts the erythrocyte into the rigid sickle shape shown in panel b, causing hemolysis and vaso-occlusion.

questions.stem: An adult presents with the yellowish eyelid deposits, corneal arcus, and tendon nodules over the knuckles and Achilles tendon shown here. These findings together most strongly suggest what underlying abnormality?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Markedly elevated LDL cholesterol, as in familial hypercholesterolemia

explanation.summary: Xanthelasma, corneal arcus, and tendon xanthomas are lipid-laden macrophage deposits seen with high circulating LDL. Their presence, especially at a young age, points to familial hypercholesterolemia (LDL-receptor defect).
explanation.whyCorrect: Xanthelasma, corneal arcus, and tendon xanthomas are lipid-laden macrophage deposits seen with high circulating LDL. Their presence, especially at a young age, points to familial hypercholesterolemia (LDL-receptor defect).

questions.stem: This heme synthesis pathway shows lead (Pb) blocking ferrochelatase. Because iron can no longer be inserted into the ring, which molecule accumulates and serves as a clinical screening marker for lead poisoning?

explanation.summary: Ferrochelatase normally inserts Fe2+ into protoporphyrin IX to make heme. Lead inhibits ferrochelatase (and ALA dehydratase), so protoporphyrin IX accumulates and zinc binds in place of iron, raising zinc/free erythrocyte protoporphyrin, a marker used to screen for lead toxicity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ferrochelatase normally inserts Fe2+ into protoporphyrin IX to make heme. Lead inhibits ferrochelatase (and ALA dehydratase), so protoporphyrin IX accumulates and zinc binds in place of iron, raising zinc/free erythrocyte protoporphyrin, a marker used to screen for lead toxicity.
